opp.next_step: Marc to share the Zycus deck and modular AI capability overview with Justin Chang. Justin Chang to review internally with the portfolio procurement leadership team post-Memorial Day. Schedule a follow-up discussion in 3–4 weeks focused on intake orchestration, sourcing automation, and Oracle/SAP integration feasibility across portfolio companies. Request 1–2 operational inputs from Roark Capital (e.g., sourcing volume, ERP landscape, auto-renewal trends) to tailor an ROI-driven demo and discussion.
opp.ais_why:
Justin Chang showed interest in Zycus’ agentic AI approach, particularly beyond traditional LLM-based procurement automation.
The team was engaged by the intake-to-outcome workflow vision, including autonomous sourcing and negotiation capabilities.
Interest increased around modular AI agents that can integrate with existing Oracle, SAP, and Coupa environments without requiring a full platform replacement.

sources: Avoma introductory call 20 May 2026, Justin Chang (Head of Supply Chain, Roark Capital)
SF Next_Step__c: 'Request 1–2 operational inputs from Roark Capital (e.g., sourcing volume, ERP landscape, auto-renewal trends) to tailor an ROI-driven demo and discussion'
metrics.narrative: Justin Chang articulated clear operational challenges but has not quantified financial impact. On the 20 May call he described lean procurement orgs across portfolio brands leading to auto-renewals on long-tail categories, ad hoc sourcing events, and maverick spend due to bandwidth constraints. He stated portfolio companies vary in source-to-contract and procure-to-pay sophistication, with rogue/out-of-contract spend common. He also noted a need for unified spend visibility and ML-based spend cube cleaning to identify cost-saving themes across the portfolio. However, no savings target, headcount reduction, cycle-time improvement, or ROI figure was named. The pain is operational (bandwidth, compliance, visibility) rather than a burning financial imperative—Justin said explicitly 'there's no flashing red light right now.' To advance MEDDPICC, the recommended move is to co-develop a quantified business case with Justin and one portfolio CPO, using actual sourcing volume, ERP landscape, and auto-renewal trends from a pilot brand to model ROI and time savings.

sources: SF Next_Step__c (Justin to review internally and schedule follow-up)
Account contact list (Justin Chang, Head of Supply Chain)
champion.narrative: Justin Chang (Head of Supply Chain, Roark Capital portfolio operations) is the developing champion. He initiated the conversation after seeing the Merlin demo at the ISM iCentro event in May 2026, reached out to Zycus, and is driving internal exploration. On the 20 May call he demonstrated clear understanding of the agentic AI value proposition, asked detailed questions about Oracle/SAP integration and modular deployment, and articulated a concrete next step (internal review with portfolio procurement team, followed by CPO demo in 3-4 weeks). He has access to portfolio CPOs and can convene them for a demo, and he reports to Roark's senior leadership. However, his role is advisory—he does not control budget or sign contracts—so his advocacy must translate into a CPO or CFO champion at a pilot brand to move the deal forward. His engagement is active: he committed to review the deck post-Memorial Day and schedule the follow-up. What is missing: (1) evidence of internal advocacy (has he already pitched Zycus to a CPO or CFO?), (2) access to or relationship with the economic buyer, (3) a co-authored mutual action plan with milestones. The recommended move is to deepen the champion relationship by co-developing the follow-up agenda with Justin, naming 1-2 pilot brands and their CPOs, and asking him to make the introduction so we can build a second champion at the brand level.

sources: Avoma call 20 May 2026 (Oracle named as current ERP/procurement system; Coupa referenced by Marc as integration example; Justin's ISM goal was to compare vendors)
competition.narrative: Competition is partially visible. Justin mentioned that Roark's portfolio companies currently use a variety of tools, and specifically named Oracle as the internal ERP and procurement system at Roark Capital itself. On the 20 May call, Marc (Zycus) referenced Coupa by name when explaining that the Zycus intake agent can integrate with external platforms like Coupa, and Justin acknowledged the comment without objection, suggesting Coupa may be in use at one or more portfolio brands. Justin also stated his evaluation goal at ISM was to understand 'what can we expect from procurement agent tech' and 'what are the differentiators between the players out there'—implying he is or will be comparing multiple vendors. However, no competitor was named by Justin as actively being evaluated alongside Zycus, and the Salesforce Competitors__c field is empty. The competitive wedge Zycus is positioning is the homegrown, agentic Merlin platform (agents that perform functions autonomously, not just LLM query/response) versus 'LLM-only offerings' and point solutions stitched together. Marc highlighted Gartner Magic Quadrant leadership and IDC recognition to build credibility. The recommended move is to ask Justin directly on the next call whether he is evaluating other vendors in parallel, and if so, who—and to tailor the demo to demonstrate the agentic AI differentiation (Anna autonomous negotiation, intake orchestration, AI risk radar) against those specific competitors.

sources: Avoma call 20 May 2026, Justin Chang (Head of Supply Chain, Roark Capital portfolio operations)
identify_pain.narrative: Justin Chang identified specific operational pain on the 20 May call, though he caveatted that there is 'no flashing red light'—no urgent crisis—across the portfolio. The pain is chronic bandwidth and compliance erosion rather than acute failure. First, lean procurement teams: even a $20B+ brand like Subway has only a few hundred headquarters employees and a small procurement org, creating severe bandwidth constraints. Second, ad hoc sourcing and auto-renewals: long-tail categories never get robust sourcing events due to lack of capacity, leading to auto-renewals and forgone savings. Third, mixed centralized/distributed procurement models: some spend is managed centrally, but a large tail is unmanaged or out of procurement's purview, creating maverick/rogue spend. Fourth, inconsistent procure-to-pay governance and system sophistication across brands, leading to out-of-contract spend and low compliance. Fifth, messy, fragmented spend data: Roark's portfolio operations team has built internal ML tools to clean spend cubes and identify themes, but this is labor-intensive and reactive. Justin is the pain owner; he oversees supply chain and procurement across the portfolio and is tasked with finding scalable, AI-driven solutions to relieve CPO bandwidth and improve compliance without requiring centralized control or system standardization. The pain is well-articulated and owned; the gap is quantification (see Metrics).

sources: Avoma call 20 May 2026, Justin Chang (stated Roark does not sign on behalf of portfolio companies)
paper_process.narrative: Paper process (MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec) is unknown. Justin stated that Roark Capital does not sign contracts on behalf of portfolio companies—each brand is legally independent and 'super portable' for potential divestiture—so any Zycus contract would be signed by an individual portfolio company (or potentially by Roark for an internal/advisory-only engagement). No mention of legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement approval process was made on the 20 May call. Implementation scoping, SI/partner involvement, and contract redline cycles have not been discussed. This will become material once a pilot is scoped; the recommended move is to ask Justin whether Roark has a standard vendor onboarding/InfoSec review process, or whether that responsibility sits with each portfolio brand, and to request an introduction to the relevant legal/procurement contact at the pilot brand.

sources: Salesforce account contacts (Tracy Haas CFO, Sunil Rajan VP IT) not yet engaged
Zero OpportunityContactRole records on this opp
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic buyer is not yet identified. Justin Chang leads supply chain and procurement for the portfolio operations group but explicitly stated he does not control direct spend decisions for portfolio companies—those decisions rest with individual brand CPOs and CFOs—and that he does not control Roark Capital's internal IT/procurement budget (he said 'I'd have to ask our CFO or CIO' regarding Roark's own systems). His role is advisory and value-creation project lead, not budget holder. The 20 May call had no CFO, CIO, or brand-level CPO participation. Justin mentioned Tracy Haas (CFO) and named multiple portfolio CPOs, but none have been engaged. Budget authority for a portfolio-wide or single-brand Zycus deployment likely sits with (a) Roark Capital CFO/CIO for a centralized play, or (b) an individual portfolio company CFO/CPO for a brand-level pilot. The recommended move is to ask Justin explicitly who holds budget approval for a pilot deployment at one portfolio brand, and to request an introduction to that person and the brand CPO in the next call.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is taking shape but not yet fully mapped. Justin Chang will review the Zycus deck internally with the relevant stakeholder portfolio procurement leadership team post-Memorial Day (late Jun 2026), then schedule a follow-up discussion in 3-4 weeks (early to mid-Jul). That follow-up will focus on integration feasibility and likely include a demo with one or more portfolio CPOs. Justin's role is gatekeeper and advisor; he will vet the solution and build internal consensus before escalating to a CFO, CIO, or brand-level executive sponsor for budget approval. The process appears to be: (1) Justin's internal review and alignment with portfolio procurement team, (2) deeper demo and use-case validation with CPOs from 1-2 pilot brands, (3) ROI workshop and pilot scoping, (4) executive sponsor sign-off at Roark or brand level, (5) contracting. No formal RFP, vendor shortlist, or procurement committee has been mentioned; this is an advisory-led, relationship-driven evaluation. The approval chain (Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, etc.) is not documented in Salesforce. The recommended move is to ask Justin on the next call to map the approval path explicitly: who must say yes for a pilot, and what milestones must be hit before that ask.

sources: SF Next_Step__c: 'Schedule a follow-up discussion in 3–4 weeks focused on intake orchestration, sourcing automation, and Oracle/SAP integration feasibility across portfolio companies'
decision_criteria.narrative: Justin Chang articulated explicit evaluation criteria on the 20 May call. First, modular deployment capability: he wants the ability to select discrete AI-driven S2P modules—intake orchestration, sourcing automation, CLM, risk monitoring—rather than a full-suite obligation, so portfolio brands can adopt only what fits their maturity and need. Second, Oracle and SAP ERP integration: prebuilt connectors are required to unify spend data across the heterogeneous portfolio ERP landscape without custom development. Third, agentic AI differentiation: he is evaluating whether Zycus's homegrown agentic Merlin platform (agents that perform functions autonomously, not just LLM query/response) offers a material advantage over 'AI-first' competitors. Fourth, fit for a franchise/franchisor model: the solution must handle centralized corporate G&A, company-owned store spend, and advisory/visibility use cases without requiring Roark to sign contracts on behalf of franchisees. Fifth, he seeks proof that the intake agent can embed in Microsoft Teams (confirmed on the call as used internally and by most portfolio companies). These criteria are confirmed and documented; the next step is to demonstrate Oracle/SAP integration in a live environment and show modular pricing/deployment in a formal proposal.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is AI Curious, leaning toward AI Hungry. Justin initiated contact after seeing the Merlin demo at ISM, explicitly sought to understand agentic AI differentiation, and articulated a clear use case for AI-driven intake-to-outcome automation to relieve CPO bandwidth and improve compliance across the portfolio. He stated interest in 'agents perform functions like negotiation autonomously' and asked detailed questions about modular AI deployment and ERP integration. However, he also stated 'no flashing red light'—no urgent crisis—suggesting this is strategic exploration rather than emergency buying urgency. The AIS fields in Salesforce are empty (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c all null), so there is no conflicting internal AI excitement rating. Based on the call, Justin and Roark Capital are AI Curious with a concrete AI automation use case (maverick spend sourcing, intake orchestration), and likely to become AI Hungry once a quantified ROI case is built.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 27 May 2026, 30 days old. Introductory call completed 20 May (7 days before opp creation, likely logged retroactively). Marc committed to send the Zycus deck via email post-call; this is documented in Next_Step__c and was due immediately after 20 May. No Salesforce task or activity records a follow-up email send date, but LastActivityDate is 11 Jun (~15 days ago), suggesting follow-up activity occurred. Buyer (Justin Chang) committed to internal review with portfolio procurement team post-Memorial Day (27 May US holiday) and to schedule a follow-up discussion in 3-4 weeks (target mid-to-late Jun or early Jul). As of today (26 Jun), the follow-up is due imminently. No stage progression, amount change, or additional buyer engagement has been logged since creation. Deal pulse is LIVE (activity 15 days ago, rep-initiated outreach within cycle norms for an early advisory conversation).

items.detail: Single-threaded to an advisory champion (Justin Chang) with no budget authority. No CPO, CFO, CIO, or brand-level executive engaged. Justin is overdue on internal review (due early Jun, now 26 Jun) and has not yet scheduled the follow-up demo. If he does not re-engage within 7 days, the deal becomes a ghost.

Oracle/SAP integration feasibility is Justin's top decision criterion, but Zycus has not yet demonstrated it in a live portfolio environment. If the next demo is slides-only (not a working integration proof), the deal will stall on 'we need to validate with the relevant stakeholder portfolio uses heterogeneous ERPs (Oracle, SAP, NetSuite mentioned); a generic integration demo may not satisfy.

Budget authority is unmapped. Justin does not control spend; individual portfolio brand CFOs or CPOs likely hold budget for a pilot. Roark CFO (Tracy Haas) or CIO (Sunil Rajan) may hold budget for a centralized play, but neither has been engaged. No budget discussion or approval process has been documented.

Close date May 2027 is 323 days out, consistent with a long enterprise cycle, but the deal is only 30 days old and still in Qualified (discovery). If the CPO demo does not happen in early Jul as planned, and pilot scoping does not begin by Aug/Sep 2026, the deal will not progress through shortlist/vendor selection/negotiation in time to close by May 2027. The timeline is plausible but fragile—any multi-month stall in H2 2026 will force a slip.

No urgent pain. Justin stated 'no flashing red light right now'—the pain is chronic (bandwidth, maverick spend) but not acute. This is a strategic, exploratory evaluation, not an emergency buy. Without a quantified ROI case and a committed pilot brand, the deal can drift indefinitely as a 'nice to have.'

confidence_signals.summary: Confidence signals are mixed. Positive: Justin initiated contact, engaged deeply on the 20 May call, committed to internal review and follow-up, and articulated clear decision criteria. Negative: he is overdue on the internal review (due early Jun, now 26 Jun), has not scheduled the follow-up demo, and is the only engaged stakeholder (single-threaded). LastActivityDate 11 Jun (~15 days ago) suggests some follow-up occurred, but no buyer reply or forward progress is logged. The deal is LIVE per the pulse (activity within cycle norms for early advisory conversations) but at risk of cooling if Justin does not re-engage within the next 7 days. The recommended move is immediate re-engagement to confirm internal alignment and schedule the CPO demo.

north_star_verdict.math: Deal is 30 days old (created 27 May 2026), in Qualified stage, 323 days to close (15 May 2027). Last verified activity 11 Jun (~15 days ago). Introductory call completed 20 May; deck shared per next step; follow-up scheduled post-Memorial Day (late Jun/early Jul) aligns with the planned 3-4 week cadence. No slip required; standard early-cycle pacing for an enterprise advisory-to-deployment conversation at a PE portfolio operations level.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy best practices for this early-stage, advisory-led PE portfolio deal.

competitive_position.summary: Competition is partially known. Oracle is the incumbent ERP and procurement system at Roark Capital (stated 20 May call); it is not a direct S2P competitor but represents the integration challenge and potential inertia (internal IT may favor Oracle-native procurement modules). Coupa was named by Zycus (Marc) as an example integration partner, and Justin did not object or clarify non-use, suggesting Coupa may be deployed at one or more portfolio brands; threat level is unknown (could be incumbent, could be coexistence, could be evaluating). No other vendor (Ariba, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) has been named. Justin's stated evaluation goal at ISM was to compare 'players out there' and understand AI differentiation, implying a multi-vendor evaluation is likely or planned, but he has not disclosed the shortlist. Zycus is positioned on homegrown agentic AI (Merlin agents that act, not just LLM chat), Magic Quadrant leadership, and Oracle/SAP prebuilt integration; the wedge is 'intake to outcome autonomy vs. LLM-only copilots.' The single strongest current threat is UNKNOWN—Justin has not named an active competitor. Recommended move: ask Justin directly on the next call whether he is evaluating other vendors, and tailor the demo to show agentic differentiation against them.

competitors.name: Oracle (incumbent ERP/procurement system at Roark Capital)
competitors.quote: Probably Oracle. I'll be honest with you, I don't think I fully know.
competitors.how_we_win: Oracle is ERP, not AI-driven S2P. Win by demonstrating prebuilt Oracle integration (no rip-and-replace) and agentic automation Oracle cannot deliver (Merlin intake, Anna negotiation, AI risk radar). Position Zycus as the AI layer on top of Oracle, not a replacement.

competitors.name: Coupa (potential incumbent or coexistence at portfolio brands)
competitors.quote: Acknowledged potential coexistence with Coupa, as Zycus intake agent can integrate with external platforms like Coupa.
competitors.source: Avoma call 20 May 2026 (Marc stated integration capability; Justin did not object)
competitors.how_we_win: If Coupa is deployed, position modular coexistence: Zycus Merlin Intake agent can sit on Coupa, and Zycus iSource + Anna can deliver agentic sourcing automation Coupa lacks. Highlight homegrown AI vs. Coupa's LLM-only approach. Demo the Anna autonomous negotiation agent as the killer differentiator—Coupa has no equivalent.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong and resonating. Justin explicitly sought out Zycus after seeing the Merlin demo at ISM, stated his evaluation goal was to understand AI differentiation ('what's unique, what are the differentiators between AI-first players'), and expressed curiosity about agentic AI ('agents perform functions like negotiation autonomously, beyond a standard LLM demo'). On the 20 May call, Marc successfully positioned Zycus's homegrown agentic Merlin platform (intake orchestration, Anna autonomous negotiation, AI risk radar) against LLM-only competitors, and Justin engaged deeply on modular AI deployment and Teams embedding. No AI skepticism or pushback was voiced. The Merlin narrative is landing; the next step is to demonstrate it live (Anna negotiation in action, intake agent in Teams) in the CPO demo to convert interest into proof.

items.quote: You can pick and choose which of those wedges you want to use. It is modularly based.
items.criterion: Modular deployment (select discrete AI modules—intake, sourcing, CLM, risk—not full suite)

items.quote: We have prebuilt hooks into the SAPs of the world, the Oracles of the world, all the major players, and we have 1,121 APIs built into our solution.
items.criterion: Oracle and SAP ERP integration (prebuilt connectors to unify spend data across portfolio)

items.quote: Interest in AI-powered intake-to-outcome workflows where agents perform functions like negotiation autonomously. Curiosity about Zycus's agentic AI approach to automated sourcing events beyond a standard LLM demo.
items.criterion: Agentic AI (agents that perform functions autonomously, not just LLM query/response)

items.quote: We've embedded it in Teams. The intake agent is embedded in Teams, and it conversationally guides you through the buy.
items.criterion: Microsoft Teams embedding for intake agent (conversational guided workflows)

items.quote: Each module can sit within a portfolio company independently; Roark does not sign contracts on behalf of brands.
items.source: Avoma call 20 May 2026, Justin Chang and Marc (Zycus)
items.criterion: Franchise/franchisor model fit (no requirement for Roark to sign contracts on behalf of portfolio companies)

items.quote: Schedule a follow-up discussion in 3–4 weeks focused on intake orchestration, sourcing automation, and Oracle/SAP integration feasibility across portfolio companies.
items.criterion: Integration feasibility demonstration in a live portfolio Oracle/SAP environment

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations are well-aligned with Zycus capabilities, with one unconfirmed integration criterion. Justin's stated needs—modular deployment, Oracle/SAP integration, agentic AI automation, Microsoft Teams embedding, franchise/franchisor model fit—map directly to Zycus's product positioning. The 20 May call confirmed modular sales capability, prebuilt ERP hooks, and Teams embedding. The open question is Oracle/SAP integration depth in a live heterogeneous portfolio environment (Justin requested a follow-up focused on integration feasibility). Zycus is well-positioned; the next step is proof of integration in a pilot brand's Oracle or SAP instance.

hard.ais_why:
Justin Chang showed interest in Zycus’ agentic AI approach, particularly beyond traditional LLM-based procurement automation.
The team was engaged by the intake-to-outcome workflow vision, including autonomous sourcing and negotiation capabilities.
Interest increased around modular AI agents that can integrate with existing Oracle, SAP, and Coupa environments without requiring a full platform replacement.
hard.next_step:
• Marc to share the Zycus deck and modular AI capability overview with Justin Chang.
  • Justin Chang to review internally with the portfolio procurement leadership team post-Memorial Day.
  • Schedule a follow-up discussion in 3–4 weeks focused on intake orchestration, sourcing automation, and Oracle/SAP integration feasibility across portfolio companies.
  • Request 1–2 operational inputs from Roark Capital (e.g., sourcing volume, ERP landscape, auto-renewal trends) to tailor an ROI-driven demo and discussion.

pulse.summary: Live: last verified Salesforce activity 15 day(s) ago (2026-06-11).

deltas.subject: Multi-thread immediately to portfolio brand CPO and CFO. Justin Chang is an advisory champion without budget authority; the deal cannot advance to pilot without engaging a brand-level CPO (who owns the pain and use case) and CFO/CIO (who controls budget). Justin committed to internal review post-Memorial Day and is overdue (26 Jun, review was due early Jun). The rank-1 move is to re-engage Justin, confirm his internal alignment, and secure an introduction to 1-2 pilot brand CPOs for the follow-up demo in early Jul. A single-threaded advisory deal at 323 days to close is a ghost risk—expand the stakeholder map now.

deltas.subject: Build a quantified, brand-specific business case before the CPO demo. Justin articulated operational pain (bandwidth, maverick spend, auto-renewals) but no financial impact. He explicitly requested operational inputs (sourcing volume, ERP landscape, auto-renewal trends) to tailor an ROI-driven demo. Co-develop a savings model with Justin using data from one pilot brand (e.g., Subway or another named portfolio company) BEFORE the CPO call—walk in with a credible ROI story (e.g., 'automate 40% of long-tail sourcing events, relieve 0.5 FTE, save $200k annually') so the CPO sees immediate value, not just a platform tour. deltas.subject: Preempt Oracle/Coupa coexistence objections with live integration proof. Justin's top criterion is Oracle/SAP integration feasibility, and Coupa may already be deployed at portfolio brands. The next demo must show live Oracle/SAP connectivity (spend data unification, ERP-triggered workflows) and Coupa coexistence (Merlin Intake agent embedded on Coupa, or iSource/Anna running alongside Coupa Sourcing). If Zycus presents slides instead of a working integration in a portfolio-like environment, the deal will stall on 'we need to validate with the relevant stakeholder a sandbox demo that mirrors Roark's heterogeneous ERP reality, and bring a solutions architect to the CPO call to answer integration depth questions in real time.

Highlight homegrown AI vs. Coupa's LLM-only approach. Demo the Anna autonomous negotiation agent as the killer differentiator—Coupa has no equivalent.
